description: The provided image displays a generic 'Check for updates' button icon featuring a red bookmark symbol within a circular border, which does not contain any scientific data or material characterization content readable for analysis. Based on the accompanying text extraction summary, the paper investigates Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C austenitic low-density steels with Ni and Cr additions (specifically 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr compositions) processed via homogenization at 1200°C, hot rolling with 95% reduction to 4 mm, and ultrafast cooling at ~40°C/s. The study characterizes fully recrystallized microstructures comprising equiaxed austenitic grains with high fractions of Σ3 twin boundaries, nanoscale LRO domains (~1.75–2.14 nm), and coherent κ-carbides (5.46–10.69 nm), alongside mechanical properties including yield strengths of 969–1202 MPa and elongations of 34.1–54.4%. However, without the actual scientific figure image, specific visual observations of micrographs, diffraction patterns, or mechanical curves remain unverifiable.

This figure presents a comprehensive microstructural characterization of four Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C austenitic low-density steels with varying Ni and Cr additions: 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr. The top rows display EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) maps (a1–d1) and phase maps (a2–d2) revealing equiaxed fully-recrystallized single-phase FCC austenite (γ) with average grain sizes ranging from 3.51 μm to 8.95 μm. The middle rows show TEM bright-field images (e–h) confirming single-phase austenitic structures, and dark-field images (i–l) taken from {110} LRO or {110} κ reflections revealing distinct nanoscale precipitates: 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels (high Cr content) contain homogeneously distributed nanosized long-range ordering (LRO) domains (~1.75–2.14 nm), while 2Ni and 2Ni3Cr steels (high Ni content) exhibit orthorhombic κ-carbides with cuboidal morphology, with the 2Ni steel showing the largest average size (~10.69 nm). The bottom row presents selected area diffraction patterns (m–p) recorded along the [011]γ zone axis, showing faint superlattice spots at 1/2{200}γ positions for LRO-containing steels and distinct κ-carbide superlattice reflections for Ni-rich steels, confirming the atomic ordering. These results demonstrate that Cr addition suppresses κ-carbide formation in favor of LRO domains, whereas Ni promotes κ-carbide precipitation, providing critical evidence for the alloying synergy strategy.

This X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern displays the phase constitution of four Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C-based austenitic low-density steels: 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr, which were homogenized at 1200°C for 2 h, hot-rolled with 95% reduction, and ultrafast-cooled at ~40°C/s. All four curves exhibit characteristic diffraction peaks of the face-centered cubic (FCC) γ-austenite phase, indexed as γ(111), γ(200), γ(220), γ(311), and γ(222), confirming that all alloys maintain a single-phase austenitic structure regardless of Ni and/or Cr addition. Notably, no diffraction peaks corresponding to the body-centered cubic (BCC) B2 phase are detected in any of the samples. The similar peak positions and relative intensities across all four compositions indicate that the synergistic addition of Ni and Cr does not alter the fundamental crystal structure of the matrix, supporting the conclusion that these alloying elements dissolve into the austenitic solid solution without forming secondary intermetallic phases detectable by XRD.

This figure presents high-resolution TEM characterization of nanoscale precipitates in Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels with varying Ni and Cr additions (3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr). Panels (a, c, e, g) display atomic-resolution HRTEM images along the [011]γ zone axis, with corresponding FFT patterns (insets) showing superlattice reflections from ordered structures. Panels (b, d, f, h) show inverse FFT (IFFT) images obtained using {011} and {100} superlattice reflections, where yellow ellipses highlight LRO domains in 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels and κ-carbides in 2Ni and 2Ni3Cr steels. The IFFT images reveal ellipsoidal morphologies for LRO domains and cuboidal shapes for κ-carbides. Panel (i) presents a bar chart comparing average precipitate sizes: approximately 10.69 nm for 2Ni, 5.46 nm for 2Ni3Cr, 2.14 nm for 2Ni5Cr, and 1.75 nm for 3Cr steel, demonstrating that increasing Cr content progressively reduces precipitate size while Ni promotes κ-carbide formation.

Figure 4 presents the tensile properties of Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels with varying Ni and Cr contents (3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr). Panel (a) displays engineering stress–strain curves, showing that 2Ni steel achieves the highest yield strength (YS) of 1202 MPa and ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of 1283 MPa but lower ductility (~34.1%), while 3Cr steel exhibits the lowest strength (YS 969 MPa, UTS 1141 MPa) with higher elongation (~54.4%). The 2Ni3Cr steel achieves a balanced performance with YS ~1110 MPa, UTS ~1206 MPa, and total elongation (TEL) ~45.9%. Panel (b) shows corresponding true stress–strain curves. Panel (c) illustrates strain hardening rate curves versus true strain, identifying three distinct stages (I, II, III) marked by hollow dots: Stage I features a sharp decrease in hardening rate, Stage II shows an increasing trend (prolonged with increasing Cr content), and Stage III exhibits a final decrease. Notably, 2Ni5Cr maintains higher work hardening rates than 2Ni and 2Ni3Cr in Stage III. Panel (d) compares YS versus the TS×TEL product (tensile strength × total elongation) against literature data for Fe-Al, Fe-Mn, Fe-Mn-C, Fe-Mn-Al-C steels and stainless steels, demonstrating that the present Ni&Cr-alloyed steels (red stars) achieve superior strength-ductility synergy with TS×TEL values reaching ~62.07 GPa·%, particularly exhibiting significantly enhanced yield strength at equivalent GPa·% levels compared to conventional lightweight steels.

This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of fracture surfaces from Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C austenitic low-density steels alloyed with varying Ni and Cr contents, specifically (a) 3Cr, (b) 2Ni, (c) 2Ni3Cr, and (d) 2Ni5Cr specimens after ultrafast cooling treatment. Each panel displays high-magnification views of the fibrous fracture zone alongside insets showing the macroscopic fracture surface morphology. All four steels exhibit characteristic dimple patterns across their fracture surfaces, indicating ductile fracture modes regardless of specific alloying combinations. The micrographs reveal numerous equiaxed dimples of varying sizes and depths distributed throughout the fibrous zones, consistent with microvoid coalescence mechanisms. While the 2Ni steel shows relatively finer and shallower dimples correlating with its lower elongation of 34.1%, the 3Cr, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr steels display deeper, more developed dimple structures corresponding to their superior ductility ranging from 45.9% to 54.4% elongation. These fractographic observations demonstrate that the synergy of Ni and Cr alloying successfully maintains ductile fracture behavior despite significant precipitation strengthening from nanoscale κ-carbides or long-range ordered domains.

This figure presents a comprehensive TEM investigation of the microstructural evolution in Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C-3Cr (3Cr) austenitic low-density steel during tensile deformation at various engineering strains. The bright-field images were captured along the [011]γ zone axis of the FCC matrix, with selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns inset confirming the crystal structure. Subfigures (a)-(c) at 3% strain reveal planar dislocation slip characterized by dislocation multipoles localized within {111} slip bands and constrained dislocations within (1-11) planar slip bands, indicating strong slip planarity with a Schmid factor of 0.52. Subfigures (d)-(f) at 10% strain demonstrate increased dislocation density with narrower slip band spacing, showing the operation of multiple slip systems including (1-11) and (11-1) slip bands carrying high-density dislocation arrays (HDDA). Subfigures (g)-(l) near final failure (54% strain, labeled LD) exhibit high-density dislocations, very fine slip bands, and the late formation of deformation twins (~dozen nanometres width) and stacking faults (~dozen nanometres width) observed via bright-field, high-resolution, and dark-field imaging. The evolution demonstrates that 3Cr steel is dominated by HDDA and dislocation slip throughout deformation, with strain hardening primarily contributed by HDDA densification and dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR), while deformation twins appear only at very high strain levels near fracture with limited contribution to overall ductility.

This figure presents transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showing the microstructural evolution of Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C-2Ni austenitic low-density steel at various engineering strains. Subfigures (a-b) display the microstructure at 5% strain, revealing (111) traces and planar slip bands with corresponding [011] zone axis selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns. Subfigures (c-g) capture the deformation features at 10% strain, showing the multiplication and reduced spacing of slip bands, with both bright-field and dark-field images illustrating dislocation arrangements. Subfigures (h-l) show the microstructure near final failure (labeled LD), exhibiting high dislocation densities, very fine slip bands, and occasional stacking faults (indicated by arrows), with SAED patterns confirming the austenite matrix. The images demonstrate that 2Ni steel deforms primarily via planar slip without deformation twins, contrasting with Cr-alloyed counterparts, and reveal the transition from initial planar dislocation activity to dynamic recovery and limited stacking fault formation at very high strains.

This figure presents transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the 2Ni steel (Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C-2Ni) sample close to final failure, revealing the nanoscale precipitation characteristics of κ-carbides. Subfigure (a) displays a dark-field image with a scale bar of 30 nm, showing the spatial distribution and cuboidal morphology of κ-carbide precipitates within the austenitic γ-matrix; the bright contrast indicates coherent precipitates distributed throughout the matrix. Subfigure (b) provides a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image with a scale bar of 10 nm, capturing the atomic-scale lattice structure of the κ-carbides and confirming their crystallographic features. These images collectively demonstrate that the 2Ni steel contains nanoscale κ-carbides with an average size of approximately 10.69 nm, which are reported to precipitate homogeneously in the γ-matrix due to Ni addition lowering the formation energy. The microstructural evidence supports the conclusion that these coherent κ-carbides act as strong obstacles to dislocation motion via precipitation strengthening mechanisms (ordering, coherency, and modulus mismatch), contributing to the high yield strength (1202 MPa) but relatively lower ductility (34.1% elongation) compared to Cr-containing variants, as the precipitates remain stable even close to final fracture.

This figure presents four crystal structure models illustrating the atomic configurations relevant to Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C austenitic lightweight steels. Panel (a) depicts the disordered γ-austenite phase with Fe/Mn (yellow) and Al (blue) atoms randomly occupying the vertex and face-center positions of the face-centered cubic (FCC) lattice. Panel (b) shows the ordered γ-austenite structure where Al atoms occupy vertex positions while Fe and Mn atoms occupy face-center positions. Panel (c) displays the κ-carbide perovskite structure (E2₁-type), formed when C atoms (gray) occupy the body-centered octahedral interstitial sites, with ideal stoichiometry (Fe,Mn)₃AlC. Panel (d) illustrates AlNi₃C, representing a Ni-rich κ-carbide variant where Ni atoms (cream/white) substitute for Fe/Mn positions. The color legend identifies: yellow for Fe/Mn, cream for Ni, blue for Al, and gray for C. These structures were employed in first-principles DFT calculations using the Materials Project database with GGA-PBE functional to determine formation energies and phase stability. The figure supports the core finding that Ni addition reduces formation energy (AlNi₃C: -0.181 eV/atom) promoting κ-carbide precipitation, while Cr addition increases formation energy, suppressing κ-carbide nucleation.

Figure 10 displays histograms of precipitation hardening versus precipitate radius for four Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels: (a) 3Cr, (b) 2Ni, (c) 2Ni3Cr, and (d) 2Ni5Cr. The x-axis shows precipitate radius ranging from 0.5 to 5.0 nm, while the y-axis indicates precipitation hardening magnitude from 0 to 40 MPa. Green bars represent ordering strengthening ($\sigma_{\mathrm{order}}$) and orange bars represent the sum of coherency and modulus mismatch strengthening ($\sigma_{\mathrm{coh}}+\sigma_{\mathrm{mod}}$). Red dashed lines in subfigures (a) and (d) mark the transition radius (approximately 1.1 nm and 0.9 nm, respectively) where the dominant strengthening mechanism shifts from $\sigma_{\mathrm{order}}$ to $\sigma_{\mathrm{coh}}+\sigma_{\mathrm{mod}}$. The 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels exhibit $\sigma_{\mathrm{order}}$ contributions at small radii due to LRO nanodomains below the critical size, while $\sigma_{\mathrm{coh}}+\sigma_{\mathrm{mod}}$ dominates in all alloys, particularly showing high values in 2Ni steel (peaking near 39 MPa at ~4.7 nm). These results demonstrate that Ni and Cr additions synergistically control precipitate size distributions and strengthening mechanisms, with coherency and modulus mismatch providing the primary contribution to precipitation hardening.

Figure 11 presents a stacked bar chart decomposing the calculated yield strength contributions for four Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels designated 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr. The vertical axis displays yield strength in MPa, while the colored segments represent four distinct strengthening mechanisms: lattice friction stress (σ_0, orange), grain boundary strengthening (σ_gb, green), solid solution strengthening (σ_ss, purple), and precipitation strengthening (σ_prep, yellow). Brown circular markers indicate experimental yield strength values measured by tensile tests. The chart reveals that solid solution strengthening constitutes the dominant contribution across all alloys, while precipitation strengthening varies significantly between samples, peaking in the 2Ni alloy. The sum of calculated components shows excellent agreement with experimental values, validating the strengthening model. The 2Ni sample exhibits the highest yield strength (~1200 MPa), whereas 3Cr shows the lowest (~970 MPa), demonstrating that Ni and Cr alloying synergistically enhances strength through optimized precipitation and solution hardening.

Figure 12 presents strain hardening rate versus true strain curves for Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C-3Cr (3Cr) and Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C-2Ni (2Ni) austenitic low-density steels, overlaid with schematic illustrations of deformation microstructure evolution. The 3Cr steel exhibits a higher strain hardening rate plateau (~2000 MPa) sustained to higher true strains (~0.4), whereas the 2Ni steel shows a lower plateau (~1400 MPa) with earlier failure at ~0.25 strain. Circular insets (a-f) depict the microstructural evolution: (a-c) 3Cr steel at 3%, 10%, and large deformation (LD) stages, showing progressive development from initial planar slip bands to high-density dislocation arrays (HDDA), deformation twins, and stacking faults; (d-f) 2Ni steel at 5%, 10%, and LD stages, revealing dominant planar slip with uniformly distributed slip bands that multiply and reduce spacing, accompanied by cross-slip and limited stacking faults only near fracture. The legend identifies HDDA, slip bands, deformation twins, and stacking faults. This comparison demonstrates that Cr alloying promotes dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR) and HDDA effects, prolonging the strain hardening stage and enhancing ductility, while Ni alloying favors planar slip mediated deformation, resulting in higher strength but reduced uniform elongation compared to the Cr-containing steel.
